Fixes: Frames the multi-document/multi-passage QA problem as a graph-traversal task, addressing the inability of S-shot dense retrievers to follow logical chains across documents. Predates and motivates later graph-based RAG work (MoLoRAG, MLDocRAG, MultiDocFusion) and structure-aware retrieval. The structural-question handling foreshadows the layout-as-navigation idea later used by MHier-RAG and DocAgent.
Key Technique: Builds a knowledge graph over the document corpus with passage nodes (text) and structural nodes (pages, tables) plus directed belonging edges. Three KG construction variants: TF-IDF (lexical similarity edges), KNN-ST/MDR (semantic similarity edges, optionally trained MDR-style for next-supporting-fact prediction), and TAGME (Wikipedia-entity overlap). An LLM-based KG traversal agent (instruction-tuned T5 or LLaMA) generates the next likely supporting fact and selects the closest neighbour on the graph, iterating until a budget is met. Structural questions are routed via extracted structure keywords directly to the matching nodes.
Limitations: Confined to text-only multi-document QA; ignores fine-grained visual elements (figures, charts). Quality of retrieval is upper-bounded by KG construction; denser graphs increase recall but reduce precision. Requires PDF parsing (Adobe PDF Extract). Not designed for the visually-rich multi-page DocVQA setting that later models target.

Fixes: Addresses the brittleness and rigid Top-K of static RAG (M3DocRAG, VisRAG, VDocRAG) by allowing the model to actively recover from missed retrievals via fetch_page. Also addresses the lost-in-the-middle/scaling problems of end-to-end LVLMs (Qwen2.5-VL, Docopilot, mPLUG-DocOwl2) that cannot fit very long documents. Improves on prompt-based agents (DocAgent, MDocAgent) by being trainable and OCR-free.
Key Technique: OCR-free agent built on Qwen2.5-VL-7B that frames MP-DocVQA as sequential evidence aggregation. Begins with a low-resolution thumbnail grid overview of the entire document (annotated with absolute page numbers), then alternates between three actions: retrieval_page (semantic search over unvisited pages via ColQwen), fetch_page (direct index-based fetch of high-res cached visual tokens), and answer. Structured ReAct reasoning template (analysis/relevant_pages/summary) maintains a working memory across steps. Trained with SFT on 9k expert trajectories then GRPO with composite reward (answer correctness, evidence recall, format).
Limitations: Higher inference latency and GPU memory than fixed RAG due to multi-step iteration. Bounded interaction horizon (T=8) limits very long evidence chains. Performance partially depends on the retriever (degrades modestly with weaker BM25/BGE-Large). GRPO training requires careful reward engineering.

Fixes: Directly addresses the semantic-only retrieval failure of M3DocRAG and MDocAgent, which retrieve pages with overlapping keywords but missing the actual answer. Aligns with KGP in adopting graph-based retrieval but extends it to multi-modal page graphs (KGP is text-only). Influences DFVC's cross-page-context idea by demonstrating logical relevance matters.
Key Technique: Logic-aware retrieval framework. Builds a page graph by encoding pages with ColPali and connecting pairs whose embedding similarity exceeds a threshold. A VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-3B by default) acts as the retrieval engine and traverses the graph: starting from top-w semantically scored seed pages, it iteratively scores unvisited neighbours by combining a semantic relevance score (from the doc encoder) with a logical relevance score (from the VLM, prompted to reason about question-page logical fit), until a hop budget is reached. Top-K visited pages are passed to an arbitrary LVLM for QA. MoLoRAG+ adds fine-tuning of the VLM on GPT-4o-curated relevance triplets.
Limitations: Graph construction relies on a similarity threshold (sensitive). Multi-hop traversal multiplies VLM inference calls. Logical-relevance scoring is prompt-dependent. Cannot recover from completely missing edges in the page graph.

Fixes: Targets the unimodal limitation of VisRAG/ColPali (visual only) and text-RAG with OCR (lossy parsing), which struggle when answers require both modalities. Improves over S-agent RAG (M3DocRAG) by adding evidence-chain comparison across modalities. Aligns with MDocAgent and DocLens in pursuing multi-modal multi-agent fusion, but adds explicit modality-fusion adjudication.
Key Technique: Multi-agent multimodal fusion RAG. Uses a dual-tower retrieval that runs BM25 (sparse text) and VisRAG-Ret (dense visual page retrieval) independently and returns top-K text segments + top-K visual pages. Three Qwen-based agents then collaborate: a Text Filter produces a preliminary text-grounded answer with evidence; a Visual Extractor (a VLM fine-tuned via LoRA on a knowledge-distilled MP-DocVQA dataset) produces a vision-grounded answer; a Modality Fuser performs consistency checking and synthesises the final answer.
Limitations: Two parallel retrieval pipelines double indexing cost. Modality fuser still relies on a S LVLM, so cross-modal disagreements may resolve incorrectly. Knowledge-distillation dataset is task-specific (MP-DocVQA), limiting domain transfer.

Fixes: Directly addresses the poor evidence-page recall of vector retrievers (MDocAgent/M3DocRAG: 55-71% Recall@10) and the hallucination on unanswerable questions of vanilla LVLMs. Targets the loss of detail when only full-page screenshots are inspected (problem of mPLUG-DocOwl2 and Docopilot). Outperforms SimpleDoc by adding fine-grained visual element extraction and explicit adjudication.
Key Technique: Tool-augmented multi-agent framework with two modules. The Lens Module zooms in: a Page Navigator runs a VLM with OCR text + screenshots over chunked pages (sampled multiple times) to retrieve evidence pages with near-perfect recall (~97% on MMLongBench-Doc); an Element Localizer applies layout detection and crops figures/tables/charts for fine-grained inspection. The Reasoning Module uses a sampling-adjudication strategy: an Answer Sampler generates multiple CoT candidates, an Adjudicator picks the best.
Limitations: Depends on closed-source proprietary VLMs and external layout/OCR tools; not open-source end-to-end. Sampling-adjudication multiplies inference cost. Element Localizer offers little gain on text-only questions. Page Navigator must process the full document once, which limits scalability to extremely long corpora.

Fixes: Targets the context-window and irrelevant-context problems of feeding entire documents to LVLMs (Gemini-page-images, GPT-4o-OCR baselines). Specifically beats S-modal RAG (PDFTriage, M3DocRAG) by adding multi-modal cross-verification. Establishes the agentic pattern that later DocDancer, DocLens, MDocAgent, SimpleDoc and Doc-V* refine, exposing the need for trainable open-source agents.
Key Technique: Multi-agent framework that mimics human reading: extracts a hierarchical XML outline from PDFs (sections, headings, image captions, table titles, with first-sentence hints), then an actor agent uses five tools (search, get_section_content, get_image, get_page_images, get_table_image) to selectively retrieve content. A reviewer agent cross-verifies using complementary modalities; on disagreement a reflection module updates a cumulative task-agnostic memory bank that persists across questions.
Limitations: Heavily prompt-engineered and tied to closed-source LLMs; no learned policy. Adobe PDF Extract may fail on scans or unusual layouts. Memory bank is a S growing string that can drift over time. API cost and latency are high. The reviewer uses the same base model, so cognitive blind spots may persist.

Fixes: Targets the over-engineered multi-agent pipelines (MDocAgent, ViDoRAG) by showing a S VLM agent with good retrieval can match or beat them. Improves M3DocRAG by iterating retrieval until evidence is sufficient and by re-ranking with semantic summaries instead of pure embedding similarity. Influences Doc-V*, DocLens and DocDancer in promoting iterative S-agent designs.
Key Technique: Two-stage framework. Offline: each page is indexed twice as a ColPali visual embedding and a 3-5 sentence VLM-generated semantic summary. Online: dual-cue retrieval first shortlists pages by embedding similarity then asks an LLM to re-rank/filter using the summaries. A S VLM reasoner agent inspects the retrieved pages along with a working memory (initialised with a doc-level summary) and either answers, declares unanswerable, or emits a refined query for another retrieval round (max L iterations). Memory is updated with notes from each iteration.
Limitations: Quality of pre-computed summaries bounds the re-ranker. Iterative loop adds latency. Working memory is text-only; visual evidence not preserved between rounds. Limited tool use compared to DocAgent or DocLens.

Fixes: Targets the inability of pure-text RAG (ColBERT alone) and pure-vision RAG (ColPali alone) to integrate both modalities for questions requiring cross-modal reasoning. Influential in establishing role-specialised multi-agent pipelines for DocQA; later refined by ViDoRAG, M2RAG, MACT, DocLens and SimpleDoc.
Key Technique: Five-stage multi-modal multi-agent pipeline: (1) document pre-processing extracts text via OCR and preserves page images; (2) parallel ColBERT (text) and ColPali (image) retrieval return top-K segments; (3) a general agent gives a preliminary multi-modal answer and a critical agent extracts key textual + visual cues; (4) specialised text and image agents perform focused analysis using the critical cues; (5) a summarising agent integrates all answers into a final response.
Limitations: Five-agent pipeline is computationally heavy and over-engineered; later work (SimpleDoc) shows similar accuracy with a S agent. Vector retrievers have low evidence-page recall (~71% in the DocLens analysis). Does not iterate retrieval; if both modalities miss, the system fails.

Fixes: Designed against the S-shot retrieval failure of VisRAG/ColPali when queries are under-specified or evidence is scattered across modalities (e.g., needing figures on a different page than the text mention). Influences subsequent iterative agentic frameworks (Doc-V*, DocLens, ViDoRAG, SimpleDoc) by formalising the trade-off between information gain and uncertainty.
Key Technique: Adaptive iterative ReAct-style framework that frames retrieval as InfoNCE-guided mutual-information maximisation. At each step an LLM judge decomposes the residual information gap into sub-queries and the retriever (ColPali or VisRAG) returns candidates whose normalised information gain (gain minus entropy) is greedily maximised. Stops when residual entropy cannot be reduced further.
Limitations: Requires multiple LLM calls per question, increasing latency and API cost. Information-gain estimation is approximated and can be noisy. Heavily dependent on the underlying retriever quality. Not specialised for very long context aggregation.

Fixes: Argues monolithic VLM scaling (bigger Qwen2.5-VL/InternVL3) gives diminishing returns on document-based VQA, motivating procedural scaling. Addresses cognitive overload in S-pass models (Docopilot, mPLUG-DocOwl2) and the lack of internal verification in non-agentic baselines. Improves over MDocAgent/SimpleDoc by separating judgment from correction to avoid cognitive blind spots.
Key Technique: Procedural scaling: decomposes the document workflow into four specialised agents (Planning, Execution, Judgment, Answer). The planning agent generates analogical relevant plans without execution details; the execution agent picks tools from a tool library and runs each plan step; the judgment agent independently flags incorrect plan or execution steps and routes them back for correction (no self-correction or combined judge+correct); the answer agent synthesises the final answer from both correct and corrected processes. Adds an agent-wise adaptive test-time scaling strategy with mixed reward modelling (per-agent + global outcome) so each agent gets its own scaling regime.
Limitations: Multi-agent overhead increases inference cost; up to 3 correction rounds per question. Judgment agent only flags but does not correct, so plan/execution agents must learn to repair specific errors. Performance depends on the underlying tool library quality.

Fixes: Addresses the static Top-K problem of M3DocRAG and the under-utilised reasoning of S-agent RAG. Influences (and is contemporaneous with) AVIR's adaptive selection but uses GMM rather than k-means. Tackles the gap that prior VQA datasets are not built for large-corpus RAG by providing ViDoSeek with unique answers and reference pages.
Key Technique: Multi-modal hybrid retrieval with adaptive Top-K via Gaussian Mixture Model: the GMM models retrieval-score distribution to dynamically choose how many pages to keep per query, then visual + textual pipelines are fused. Multi-Scale View Generation uses three agents: a Seeker rapidly scans thumbnails and selects relevant images; an Inspector reviews them and provides reflection + preliminary answer; an Answer Agent ensures consistency and gives the final answer. Releases ViDoSeek, the first large-scale collection of unique-answer queries across ~6k images for evaluating RAG over corpora.
Limitations: Three-agent pipeline plus GMM-based hybrid retrieval is computationally heavy. GMM fitting may misbehave on very small or very uniform score distributions. Performance hinges on consistency between agents; conflicts can lead to inferior answers.

Fixes: Addresses DocAgent and SimpleDoc's reliance on closed-source LLMs and prompt-only design by providing the first end-to-end-trained open-source DocQA agent. Tackles the scarcity of high-quality DocQA training data via the Exploration-then-Synthesis pipeline. Improves outline quality over DocAgent's Adobe PDF Extract. Directly competes with MDocAgent, MACT and ViDoRAG by showing a S trained agent matches multi-agent systems.
Key Technique: First end-to-end trained DocQA agent. Uses MinerU2.5 for high-precision XML outline construction (17 element types with layout/semantic attributes). Provides only two tools: Search (full-text keyword search returning section IDs/snippets) and Read (goal-conditioned multimodal reader powered by an auxiliary VLM, Qwen3-VL-235B by default). Trained on 5k synthesized trajectories generated by an Exploration-then-Synthesis pipeline that explores documents via tool-use to produce challenging multi-hop QA pairs. Loss masks observation tokens to focus learning on agent decisions.
Limitations: Depends on parsing quality of MinerU2.5; tool design is intentionally minimalist (only Search/Read), which may limit fine-grained spatial queries. Read tool relies on an auxiliary large VLM (Qwen3-VL-235B), so total inference cost is high. Synthetic data quality bounds learned behaviours.

Fixes: Addresses the narrow training data of LLaVAR/DocOwl (1-3 task clusters, S template) that prevented zero-shot generalisation across VDU tasks. Tackles the gap that general visual instruction-tuning datasets ignore text-rich documents. Provides the dataset and bridging-module recipe that later models like Leopard and Docopilot extend with multi-image and long-context training.
Key Technique: Releases InstructDoc, the first large-scale visual instruction-tuning collection of 30 datasets across 12 VDU task clusters with expert-written instructions covering both intent and answer style. Trains InstructDr, which extends BLIP-2 with a Document-former bridging module that fuses OCR text + layout + visual features via cross- and self-attention into m learnable tokens, then projects them into FlanT5's input space via an MLP. Updates only Document-former + projector during training; encodes multiple pages in parallel for multi-page documents.
Limitations: Per-page processing with mean-pooling for multi-page inputs limits cross-page reasoning. Most tasks in the dataset are S-page; multi-page coverage (DUDE, SlideVQA) is small. Requires off-the-shelf OCR. CLIP encoder is not document-specialised.

Fixes: Built explicitly to address RAG's fragmented retrieval, multi-stage error accumulation and extra latency (criticism aimed at M3DocRAG/CREAM-style pipelines). Provides the first large-scale high-quality multi-page document QA training corpus, filling a gap noted by InstructDoc, Hi-VT5 and Leopard. Acts as a strong native baseline that retrieval-based methods (Doc-V*, DocLens, MoLoRAG) compare against.
Key Technique: Native multi-page MLLM (no RAG) trained on a new 758k-sample Doc-750K dataset (5.2B text tokens, 3.1M images) covering S- and cross-page proxy tasks (abstract/title/caption/experiment writing, translation, multi-image QA). Uses InternVL2 architecture with engineering tricks (multimodal data packing, Ring Attention, Liger Kernel) to extend MLLM context for full-document training and inference.
Limitations: Performance bound by the LLM's context window even with Ring Attention. Visual tokens still grow linearly with document length, limiting practical scalability beyond several dozen pages. Heavy training cost and dataset construction. Lacks an explicit retrieval/agent fallback when documents exceed capacity.

Fixes: Solves the position-ID waste of prior layout-as-token methods (e.g., DocLLM, He et al. inline {text,Box:[...]}) that consumed thousands of position IDs for layout strings on MP-DocVQA, squeezing the textual learning space. Provides a lightweight alternative to layout-as-modality models (UDOP, DocFormerv2) that need extra encoders, and to OCR-free visual MLLMs (mPLUG-DocOwl2, Texthawk2) whose visual tokens scale with resolution.
Key Technique: Represents the layout of each OCR text segment as a S layout token produced by a layout tokenizer (4D-bbox projector + attention encoder over a learnable query). The layout token is interleaved after its corresponding text segment but shares the first position ID of that segment, so layout tokens consume zero extra position IDs (T-Ratio remains 100% for text). Adds a new pre-training objective NTLP (Next Interleaved Text and Layout Token Prediction) that requires cross-modal prediction between text and layout tokens.
Limitations: No visual modality, so charts/figures are lost. Heavy reliance on OCR quality and bounding boxes. Layout token shares position IDs with text, which can complicate fine-grained spatial reasoning. Requires NTLP pretraining.

Fixes: Solves Qwen2.5-VL's information dilution and evidence-localization failure on long PDFs without requiring an external retriever, addressing limitations identified in M3DocRAG/MDocAgent's retrieve-then-read pipelines (decoupled retrieval is brittle). Also targets shallow chart comprehension that hurts Docopilot/Leopard/InternVL3. Provides an end-to-end alternative to fixed RAG and agentic frameworks like SimpleDoc/MDocAgent while preserving interpretability via explicit reasoning traces.
Key Technique: Chain-of-Reading: a four-stage trainable CoT paradigm (Task Planning -> Phased & Focused Search -> Cross-modal Evidence Integration -> Synthesized Reasoning & Verification) supervised by a 26k QA CoR-Dataset with explicit reasoning traces. Adds Mask-AR, a self-supervised objective that masks figure/table captions and forces the MLLM to reconstruct them from the visual content, strengthening chart/figure grounding. Three-stage training: LoRA SFT on public DU data, full SFT on CoR+Mask-AR, then DPO alignment.
Limitations: Still bounded by the Qwen2.5-VL context window for very long documents. Requires expensive supervised CoR traces and DPO data. Mask-AR depends on scientific-paper-style figure-caption pairs, which may not transfer to slides/forms. Performance gap remains versus strongest proprietary models (GPT-4o).

Fixes: Targets the gap that prior visual RL methods (VisualRFT, Vision-R1, Video-R1) ignored multi-page document reasoning. Provides an alternative to retrieval-based pipelines (M3DocRAG, MoLoRAG) by training the MLLM itself to perform coarse-to-fine evidence selection. Complements Doc-V*'s GRPO-based agentic training with a non-agentic, S-pass formulation.
Key Technique: EviGRPO: a GRPO variant for multi-page document understanding that adds an evidence-page-aware reward to the standard format and ANLS accuracy rewards. The model must output structured <think>/<evidence_page>/<answer> triplets with per-page T/F relevance flags; the evidence reward is F1 between predicted and gold evidence pages (using F1 instead of accuracy avoids the reward-hacking trap of always predicting all pages irrelevant). Two-stage curriculum: S-page warmup then multi-page training on 4.8k EviBench examples generated by a two-stage MLLM annotation+verification pipeline; releases ArxivFullQA evaluation set.
Limitations: Limited training data (4.8k examples). Cannot scale to documents whose page count exceeds Qwen2.5-VL's image-token capacity. Reward design assumes evidence-page annotations exist (not always available). S-pass output means no iterative refinement when evidence is missed.

Fixes: Addresses the fact that prior text-rich MLLMs (LLaVAR, mPLUG-DocOwl-1.5, UReader) were optimised for S images and cannot reason across slide decks, multi-chart articles or multi-page documents. Tackles the dual challenge of resolution vs sequence length that hampered Mantis, VILA, Idefics2 and LLaVA-Next-Interleave on text-rich inputs. Complements multi-page VRDU pipelines (Docopilot, mPLUG-DocOwl2) with a recipe for multi-image instruction-tuning.
Key Technique: MLLM specialised for text-rich multi-image tasks. Curates Leopard-Instruct, a 925k instance instruction-tuning dataset including 585k newly collected multi-image samples (multi-chart from Pew, slide decks from SlideShare, etc.) generated by GPT-4o with cross-image reasoning rationales. Introduces an adaptive high-resolution multi-image encoding module that computes per-image sub-image splits based on aspect ratio and resolution under a global token budget, then applies pixel shuffling (4x token compression) before vision-language projection.
Limitations: Despite adaptive encoding, multi-page documents still produce long visual sequences. Pixel-shuffle compression is lossless geometrically but discards per-token granularity. Heavy reliance on GPT-4o-generated synthetic instructions. Limited explicit cross-page agent reasoning.

Fixes: Targets two problems other models leave open: (i) the linear visual-token growth of mPLUG-DocOwl2/Docopilot/InternVL2 that exceeds edge GPU memory, and (ii) the latency overhead of RAG pipelines like InternVL2-RAG (3.5x slower). Builds on GRAM's compression idea but maintains a constant per-page cost. Improves over LayTokenLLM by jointly compressing vision+OCR+layout in one module.
Key Technique: Compact 2B Small VLM designed for edge deployment. Hierarchical Multimodal Compressor jointly fuses visual patches, OCR text and layout into a fixed 576 tokens per page (5.6x reduction): Local OCR Compression cross-attends visual patches to spatially-aligned OCR tokens; Global Visual Compression then summarises local features into a fixed page representation using global low-res features as queries. Streaming Abstention partitions documents into segments processed sequentially; only text predictions and per-token entropy are stored across segments, then an Uncertainty Calibrator picks the most confident segment answer (with explicit "Not Answerable" abstention training).
Limitations: Compression to 576 tokens/page may lose fine details on dense text-heavy pages. Streaming aggregation is S-pass; cannot revisit earlier segments. Smaller backbone caps reasoning ability versus 7B+ models. Requires multi-stage curriculum data.

Fixes: Targets the high token cost of dual-encoder (CogAgent, MiniGemini) and large-token (UReader, TextMonkey) text-rich LVLMs. Tackles the trade-off between OCR accuracy and grounding ability that competitor LVLMs (GPT-4o, InternVL 1.5) struggle to balance. Provides a strong S-encoder OCR-free backbone alternative to mPLUG-DocOwl2 and DocSLM.
Key Technique: Bilingual LVLM with 16x visual-token compression via TextHawk's two-step resampling+rearrangement (each step reduces tokens by 4x). Visual encoder is unified SigLIP-SO400M reinforced via LVLM co-training to transfer to previously unseen tasks like Chinese OCR and grounding. Adds a detection head with special coordinate tokens (representing a bounding box in 7 tokens vs 25 for a digit string) trained jointly with an L1 loss for grounding. Dynamic high-resolution cropping (up to 72 sub-images / 1.8M pixels at SFT). Pre-trained on ~100M diverse samples.
Limitations: 16x compression may still miss extremely fine text on huge documents. Detection-head grounding requires special coordinate-token vocabulary. No explicit multi-page/agent reasoning module; multi-page support comes from sub-image cropping plus LLM context.

Fixes: Designed against the 1k-3k visual-tokens-per-page cost of UReader/Monkey/InternVL/InternLM-XComposer that prevents multi-page processing. Improves over independent per-crop compression (TokenPacker) and learnable-query compression (Q-former, Resampler) by exploiting layout structure. Sets the OCR-free multi-page baseline that DocSLM, Texthawk2, Leopard and Docopilot compare against.
Key Technique: High-resolution DocCompressor that uses cross-attention with the global low-resolution feature map as queries and the spatially-corresponding sub-image features as keys/values, compressing each high-resolution document image into 324 tokens regardless of resolution while preserving layout-aware groupings. Inserted after the H-Reducer V2T module so compression operates in the LLM's textual feature space. Three-stage training: S-image pretraining, multi-image continue-pretraining, multi-task fine-tuning.
Limitations: 324 tokens/page may still under-represent text-dense pages. Compression quality depends on the global feature map's coverage. Training pipeline is multi-stage and data-heavy. Does not retrieve or iterate; performance degrades on documents exceeding context limit.

Fixes: Targets the lack of cross-page context in Hi-VT5 (each page encoded independently before decoding) and the quadratic cost of concatenation-based methods. Provides a low-parameter alternative to GRAM's local-global attention by using recurrence instead of global tokens. Inspired by Transformer-XL and the original RMT for NLP.
Key Technique: Recurrent Memory Transformer adapted to MP-DocVQA. Each page is processed in sequence by a T5 encoder that takes as input the previous page's m memory tokens (default 100), the textual question and the current page's OCR text + DiT visual patches. The encoder updates the memory tokens, which carry context to the next page. After all pages, all per-page memory tokens are concatenated and passed to the T5 decoder for answer generation. Curriculum pre-training adds one page at a time. An answer-page-prediction module provides explainability.
Limitations: Recurrent processing means later pages have indirect access to early pages only via memory; long documents may suffer from forgetting. Memory size is fixed (100 tokens). Training requires curriculum strategy. Cannot revisit earlier pages once their memory has been overwritten.

Fixes: Targets TILT's fragile S-step text/image fusion and its 9k-token ceiling that prevented multi-page DU. Addresses the general MP-VRDU problems of quadratic attention cost and OCR token explosion, while keeping cost low enough for edge/enterprise deployment. Competes directly with hierarchical/long-context baselines like Hi-VT5 and GRAM and very large LVLMs (GPT-4V, Gemini), aiming to match them at sub-billion scale on long business documents.
Key Technique: Extends the TILT encoder-decoder by replacing one-time text+image summation fusion with layer-wise tensor-product fusion applied after every encoder block (with gating, layer norm, dropout). Adds SLED-style chunked sparse attention (block-diagonal mask), nested gradient checkpointing, mixed precision, KV-cache removal and CPU offloading so a S 24GB GPU can train and serve up to 389k input tokens (~500 pages). Trained from T5-Large with extended self-supervised pretraining on CCpdf/OCR-IDL and SFT on 17 DU datasets, plus calibrated confidence scores via per-token min-aggregation.
Limitations: Visual backbone is small (~8M params) and struggles with unusual aspect ratios, slide-heavy or infographic layouts. Performance shows a strong primacy bias toward evidence near the document start (lost-in-the-middle on later pages). Still constrained to ~500 pages and depends on OCR quality; not designed for cross-page agentic reasoning.

Fixes: Establishes the multi-page VRDU problem itself by introducing MP-DocVQA. Solves the fundamental input-truncation problem of S-page LayoutLM/T5/BigBird models while keeping per-page processing tractable. Sets the baseline that GRAM, RM-T5, Arctic-TILT, CREAM and many later models compare against and explicitly try to surpass.
Key Technique: Hierarchical T5-based encoder-decoder. Each page is independently encoded with OCR tokens (with 2D position embeddings), DiT visual patches and the question; M learnable [PAGE] tokens summarise per-page content, similar to BERT's [CLS]. The decoder consumes the concatenation of all pages' [PAGE] tokens to generate the answer. An auxiliary answer-page-prediction head predicts which page contains evidence, providing explainability. Introduces the MP-DocVQA dataset (46k questions over 6k documents up to 20 pages).
Limitations: Pages are encoded fully independently before the decoder, losing cross-page context until late fusion. Cannot scale beyond ~20 pages in practice; truncates inputs. Relies on OCR quality. The S-page-evidence assumption built into MP-DocVQA limits learning of true multi-hop reasoning.

Fixes: Targets Hi-VT5's lack of cross-page interaction prior to decoding (each page encoded fully independently) and the quadratic cost of LayoutLM/Longformer/BigBird on flat concatenated documents. Demonstrates that local+global attention can be retrofitted to strong S-page backbones without expensive pretraining, an alternative to the OCR-free MLLM trend (Docopilot, mPLUG-DocOwl2).
Key Technique: Extends a S-page DocFormerv2 encoder-decoder to multi-page by interleaving page-level (intra-page self-attention with shared weights) and document-level (global doc-token-only self-attention) sub-layers in each block. Introduces learnable Doc tokens with a per-attention-head decaying constant bias to force the pretrained encoder to attend to the new global tokens. Optional C-Former (compression transformer with N_c learnable queries) compresses concatenated encoder outputs before the decoder, trading accuracy for latency. Avoids the O((N*K)^2) cost of naive concatenation and avoids extra pretraining.
Limitations: Memory still grows linearly with page count (no retrieval gating). DocFormerv2 backbone is proprietary, hindering reproducibility. C-Former compression hurts accuracy when N_c is small. Still constrained to documents that fit in S-pass encoder+decoder.

Fixes: Addresses the problem that fixed-length / semantic chunking (LumberChunker, Perplexity chunking, StyleDFS) ignores document hierarchy and breaks evidence across chunk boundaries on long industrial documents. Complements DocAgent's outline construction and MHier-RAG's parent-page retrieval by providing a more accurate hierarchy-construction recipe.
Key Technique: Multimodal chunking pipeline with four stages: (a) Document Parsing (DETR/VGT) detects layout regions and bounding boxes; (b) OCR (Tesseract/EasyOCR/TrOCR) extracts text per region; (c) DSHP-LLM, an instruction-tuned LLM (Llama-3.2-3B / Qwen-2.5-3B / Mistral-8B with LoRA), constructs a Document Hierarchical Tree by inferring header parent-child links and attaching general nodes (tables, figures, text) by spatial order; (d) DFS-based Grouping traverses the tree to produce hierarchical chunks marked by Markdown headers, splitting when a max-length is exceeded.
Limitations: Heavy multi-stage pipeline; DP/OCR errors propagate. DSHP-LLM requires fine-tuning. Hierarchy inference can fail on poorly structured scans. Still text-centric; no native image retrieval.

Fixes: Directly addresses the dichotomy between pure-text RAG (loses visual info) and pure-vision MLLM (token explosion); positions itself between Hi-VT5/GRAM/Fox/CREAM/DocOwl2-style page-level encoding (still linear) and dense long-context LLMs. Demonstrates that joint training of retriever and generator beats fixed-pipeline RAG (M3DocRAG).
Key Technique: End-to-end sparse sampling MLLM for long PDFs with interleaved text and images. A document parser extracts text blocks and figure/table images; a sparse sampler encodes them with a text encoder and a shared image encoder, computes similarity to the user query and selects the top-K most relevant text and image evidences. Trained end-to-end with contrastive loss over positive (annotated evidence) and negative (random) samples, jointly with the LLM. Provides PaperPDF, a bilingual 1.1M-QA dataset with annotated evidence sources.
Limitations: Requires positive evidence annotations for training. Quality of document parsing bounds performance. Sampler-LLM coupling means swapping LLMs requires retraining. Sparse sampling may miss multi-evidence questions if K is small.

Fixes: Extends Doc2Query/QCG-RAG to the multimodal setting, addressing the cross-modal heterogeneity and cross-page reasoning failures of standard RAG (M3DocRAG, VisRAG). Provides an alternative to MoLoRAG (page-graph) and MHier-RAG (parent-page index) by anchoring retrieval in synthetic queries rather than chunks or pages.
Key Technique: Multimodal Chunk-Query Graph (MCQG) constructed via MDoc2Query: an LVLM generates multiple answerable query-answer pairs from each modality-specific chunk (paragraphs, figures, tables, equations). Query nodes are linked to their source chunk via Chunk-Query edges and to top-K nearest queries via Query-Query edges (KNN over query embeddings). At inference, the user query retrieves similar generated queries via KNN in vector space, then the linked chunks are aggregated and ranked for the LVLM generator. Storage decouples vector DB (FAISS) from graph DB (Neo4j).
Limitations: Heavy offline cost to generate and embed many queries per chunk. Quality depends entirely on the LVLM's query-generation quality. Visual chunks are accessed only via their queries, which can lose visual fidelity. Graph maintenance complexity for evolving corpora.

Fixes: Targets two failure modes of M3DocRAG/MDocAgent: (i) inability to retrieve visual evidence whose surrounding text on the same page is the only handle (multi-modality disconnection) and (ii) inability to aggregate evidence from non-adjacent pages (long-distance reasoning). Sits between pure RAG and agentic methods by enriching the index rather than the inference loop.
Key Technique: Hierarchical index combining (i) flattened in-page indexing of small textual chunks (text+table+layout-text) and (ii) topological cross-page indexing built by clustering semantically related text blocks and recursively summarising them with an LLM into a tree. Multi-granularity retrieval: page-level parent-page retrieval (hits a chunk then returns the entire parent page with all visual elements) for multi-modal connection, plus document-level summary retrieval over the tree for long-distance reasoning. Both stages use joint similarity + LLM re-ranking; final answer generated with a CoT prompt and structured-output template.
Limitations: Pre-processing is heavy (clustering, GMM, recursive LLM summarisation). Topological tree must be rebuilt per document. Visual content is mediated through textual descriptions, losing fidelity. Does not natively retrieve images; relies on parent-page heuristic for multi-modal evidence.

Fixes: Targets the i.i.d. page-encoding assumption of VisRAG and ColPali/ColQwen, which discards cross-page semantics when tables, paragraphs or sections span page breaks. Avoids the context-dilution problem that arises from naively concatenating neighbouring pages (as a sliding-window text RAG would). Complements graph-based and re-ranking approaches (MoLoRAG, MHier-RAG) by working purely at the embedding level.
Key Technique: Dynamically Fusing Visual Context: a lightweight MLP adapter sits on top of a frozen VLM (VisRAG or ColQwen) and predicts gating weights (alpha, beta) over the previous and next page embeddings to produce a residual-fused embedding for the current page. Trained with a list-wise contrastive ranking loss plus a drift regularizer that prevents the fused embedding from straying too far from the original. Initialised with negative bias so the adapter starts as identity mapping for stable training.
Limitations: Only fuses across a fixed window of three pages (previous/current/next); cannot capture longer-range cross-page context. Requires training the adapter per backbone. Improves retrieval but does not change the downstream generator. Limited by base VLM's per-page representation quality.

Fixes: Bridges the gap between S-page VQA and text-only RAG (DPR/BM25 + LLM), neither of which preserves visual cues across many pages. Provides the canonical multi-modal page-retrieval baseline that virtually every later RAG/agentic system (MDocAgent, MoLoRAG, ViDoRAG, SimpleDoc, Doc-V*, AVIR) compares against and tries to improve.
Key Technique: ColPali (multi-modal late-interaction visual retriever) encodes every document page into per-patch embeddings; query encoded similarly; MaxSim score selects top-K relevant pages. For open-domain settings, IVF approximate indexing is used to scale to 40k+ pages with sub-2s retrieval. The retrieved page images are passed directly to a multi-modal LM (Qwen2-VL 7B) for answer generation. Introduces the M3DocVQA open-domain benchmark (3.4k+ PDFs, 41k pages, 2.4k multi-hop questions extended from MultimodalQA).
Limitations: Fixed Top-K retrieval; brittle when relevant evidence is missed. ColPali multi-vector embeddings are storage-heavy. Generator is a S forward pass over retrieved pages; cannot revise queries. Performance plateaus around K=4 due to attention dilution.

Fixes: Addresses the S-page limitation of LayoutLM/UDOP/DocFormer and the truncation/noise problems of dense text-only RAG when applied to multi-page DocVQA. Directly improves on Hi-VT5's per-page-then-decoder design by introducing a multi-page vision encoder with cross-page attention pooling. Tackles the general MP-VRDU bottleneck of fitting multiple high-res pages into an LLM context.
Key Technique: Coarse-to-fine retrieval that first uses sentence embeddings then iteratively re-ranks chunks with RankVicuna in groups of m, narrowing to top-3. Pairs this with a multi-page vision encoder built on Pix2Struct: per-page features are augmented with page-position embeddings and merged via attention pooling with a learnable visual query, then fed to LLaMA via an AdapterMLP. LLaMA-Adapter V2-style efficient tuning (LoRA + prefix + bias) avoids full fine-tuning.
Limitations: Heavy reliance on OCR quality; iterative LLM re-ranking is expensive at inference time. Attention-pooled multi-page representation can wash out fine-grained details when many pages are retained. Frozen Pix2Struct encoder limits visual reading on novel layouts. Attention-pooling itself is a fixed aggregator (cannot dynamically inspect evidence like agentic methods).

Fixes: Addresses the limitations of conventional text-based RAG (parsing-induced information loss) and previous visual retrievers like ColPali/VisRAG (no retrieval-specific pre-training). Establishes that LVLMs need retrieval-oriented adaptation to bridge generative pre-training and retrieval. Provides the OpenDocVQA benchmark used by later open-domain methods.
Key Technique: LVLM-based dual-encoder retriever (VDocRetriever) that compresses each high-resolution document image into a S dense <EOS>-token embedding, with a generator (VDocGenerator) using the retrieved image directly. Two new self-supervised pre-training tasks transfer LVLM capabilities to retrieval: Representation Compression via Retrieval (RCR) - InfoNCE between OCR-text and image <EOS> embeddings; Representation Compression via Generation (RCG) - generate OCR text from the compressed embedding. LoRA-tunes both retriever and generator on top of Phi-3-Vision. Releases OpenDocVQA, the first open-domain DocVQA collection with multi-hop questions.
Limitations: Compressing entire image to a S <EOS> embedding loses some fine-grained information. Pre-training requires OCR (used as pseudo-targets). Performance bound by base LVLM backbone's resolution. No iterative or agentic refinement.

Fixes: Directly attacks the rigid Top-K cutoff used by SelfAttnScoring and M3DocRAG, where any retrieval error cascades into a wrong answer. Also addresses the LVLM context-dilution problem on long documents observed in Qwen2.5-VL/InternVL3 baselines, and the heavy compute of cross-encoder retrievers like ColPali. Aligns with the broader MP-VRDU trend of replacing fixed Top-K with adaptive selection (also pursued by ViDoRAG via GMM).
Key Technique: Frozen Qwen2.5-VL-3B-AWQ generator combined with a lightweight (~100M) Pix2Struct-based page retriever. Key novelty is an Adaptive Page Selector that analyses the score distribution: K-means clustering (k=2) splits relevant from irrelevant pages for documents >4 pages with a Top-8 cap, while a probability threshold (0.6) is used for short documents to avoid discarding marginally relevant pages. No fine-tuning of the LVLM; prompt engineering only.
Limitations: Retriever requires task-specific fine-tuning per dataset (limits generalization). Cannot answer global, list-of-answers or unanswerable questions well because some pages are always discarded. Hard cap of 8 pages caps recall on multi-evidence questions; cannot recover if all relevant pages are filtered out.

Fixes: Demonstrates that S-page Pix2Struct can be extended to arbitrary-length documents with minimal GPU resource usage, addressing Hi-VT5's per-page token cost and OCR dependency. Sets a lightweight baseline for retrieval-then-read that AVIR and DFVC later refine (replacing Top-1 with adaptive selection or fused embeddings).
Key Technique: OCR-free multi-page DocVQA: rendering questions as images and concatenating them on top of page images for Pix2Struct. Two-stage training: (i) train Pix2Struct on positive question-page pairs only (S-page DocVQA); (ii) freeze the encoder and train a Self-Attention Scoring module (a self-attention layer + linear head with dropout) on positive + one randomly sampled negative page per document with MSE loss + label smoothing. At inference, every page is scored independently and Top-1 is decoded by Pix2Struct. Extends MP-DocVQA evaluation to 793-page documents.
Limitations: Strict Top-1 page selection cannot answer questions whose evidence spans multiple pages. Fully visual representation requires sufficient image resolution and may miss small text. Sampling only one negative per document limits training signal. Frozen encoder caps adaptability.

Fixes: Demonstrates that simple RAG can replace concatenate-all-pages baselines and very large LVLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) on MP-DocVQA/DUDE/InfoVQA, addressing the memory-hungry inefficiency of Hi-VT5/Qwen2.5-VL on multi-page inputs. Provides a head-to-head ablation of textual vs visual RAG that complements pure-vision retrievers (M3DocRAG, VDocRAG).
Key Technique: Systematic study of RAG for multi-page DocVQA. Textual RAG variant: OCR text is chunked with overlap and tolerance, embedded with bge-en-small-v1.5 (fine-tuned via Multiple-Negatives Ranking), retrieved by cosine similarity, then re-ranked by a cross-encoder; the top-K chunks (text + bounding boxes + image crops) are concatenated and fed to VT5 or Qwen2.5-VL-7B. Visual RAG variant uses Pix2Struct's encoder to embed overlapping vertical patches of page images, retrieved via ColBERT-style late interaction; selected patches are stacked and decoded by Pix2Struct.
Limitations: Layout-guided chunking strategies were found NOT to help on these datasets (challenges the assumption that layout-aware chunking helps RAG). Visual RAG patch grids may break visual context. Cross-encoder reranking adds latency. Per-dataset fine-tuning of embedder is required."

Fixes: Targets the failure of single-modal CREAM-style OCR-text retrieval and the token-explosion problem of MLLMs that concatenate all pages (mPLUG-DocOwl2, Docopilot). Improves on Hi-VT5/GRAM by replacing decoupled per-page encoding with cross-page mixture-of-experts attention. Combines retrieval and a custom multi-page MLLM in a single trained framework rather than orchestrating frozen components.
Key Technique: Two-module framework. Hierarchical retrieval: (i) multimodal embedding similarity over page images and queries via VisRAG/ColPali for low-level matching; (ii) keyword-guided MLLM relevance scoring (InternVL2-40B prompted to output Yes/No on per-keyword relevance, with the 'Yes' logit probability used as the score) for high-level matching; (iii) LightGBM Learning-to-Rank fusion of similarity and keyword scores trained on MP-DocVQA gold pages with NDCG optimisation. DVQA module: cross-page attention-aware multi-page MLLM built on InternVL2-4B with frozen visual encoder. Each retrieved page is treated as a mixture-of-experts expert; query-conditioned attention scores combine global attention pooling and token-level attention via a gating mechanism, with top-P expert selection routing the most relevant pages to the LLM. Fine-tuned with LoRA on MP-DocVQA + DUDE training sets.
Limitations: Performance on DUDE without retrieved text context lags GRAM (visual encoder limits on complex layouts). LightGBM ranker is dataset-specific (trained on MP-DocVQA). MoE expert selection limits scalability beyond ~5 active pages. Cross-page reasoning over evidence spanning more than two pages remains weak due to insufficient training data with multi-page evidence. Hallucination on unanswerable questions persists.

Fixes: Targets the single-pass retrieval failure of M3DocRAG and MDocAgent on multi-hop questions where iterative information gathering is needed. Addresses GRPO's baseline-estimation bias for multi-turn RL: GRPO uses initial-state baseline for all intermediate states, introducing bias as trajectories diverge over turns. Improves over text-only multi-step search methods (Search-R1, DeepResearcher) by integrating both visual and textual modalities through a specialised read tool.
Key Technique: Three-agent workflow trained end-to-end with multi-turn RL. (1) Planner parses the document's table of contents (extracted via Doc2X OCR) and decomposes the query into an information-seeking plan with sub-queries and tool selections. (2) Seeker iteratively chooses between a search tool (BM25 over OCR text returning top-K snippets) and a read tool (Qwen2.5-VL-7B inspecting specified pages for visual elements), refining sub-queries based on observations until evidence is sufficient. (3) Answer agent synthesises the collected evidence into the final response. Trained with Similarity-based Policy Optimisation (SPO): the baseline for multi-turn advantage estimation is computed as a similarity-weighted average of trajectory rewards using BGE-M3 embeddings, addressing the trajectory-divergence bias of GRPO. Reward combines answer correctness (Qwen2.5-72B as judge) with read-page recall. Improves Qwen3-4B by +6.1% and Qwen3-8B by +5.0% over GRPO on MMLongBench-Doc.
Limitations: Performance partially depends on initial document parsing quality (Doc2X OCR + TOC extraction); degraded scans hurt downstream agents. Multi-turn workflow adds inference latency (~109s for 8B no-train, ~154s with SPO vs ~40s for one-pass ColQwen RAG). Optimised for static documents; dynamic/web-based documents not addressed. Validation primarily on English benchmarks; cross-lingual robustness untested. Reward design relies on Qwen2.5-72B as a judge.

Fixes: Addresses the dichotomy between document-parser RAG (which loses layout fidelity and propagates parsing errors) and concatenate-all-pages MLLMs (which exceed memory limits and dilute attention on hundreds of pages). Demonstrates that the same MLLM backbone can serve as both retriever and generator via dual LoRA adapters, eliminating the need for a separate vision-language retrieval model. Improves over CLIP/SigLIP and BGE/NV-Embed on visually-rich document retrieval by leveraging the MLLM's intermediate hidden states.
Key Technique: Self-Visual RAG framework. Retrieval module: Col-style late-interaction scoring over MLLM hidden states. The MLLM (PaliGemma, Phi-3-vision, or InternVL2-4B) embeds each page image with a fixed text prompt and the query is embedded similarly without visual input; a Col-projection layer maps last hidden states into a low-dimensional space; a MaxSim score over query-token vs page-patch features is used for ranking. Trained with contrastive InfoNCE loss using positive evidence pages and the hardest negative within the batch. QA module: classic LLaVA-style multimodal answering on top-K retrieved pages. Both modules share the same pretrained MLLM backbone with two distinct LoRA adapter sets (~2% extra parameters), enabling dual-task support without doubling memory. Releases VisR-Bench (226 documents, 471 QA pairs across 9 domains).
Limitations: Memory requirement scales with retrieved page count (up to 76GB for 12 pages of InternVL2-4B). Quality bound by base MLLM's per-page resolution; dense scientific tables can challenge the visual encoder. Fine-tuning for downstream QA can hurt MLLM generalisation (drop on MMLongBench-Doc/DUDE observed). Single-modal (visual-only) approach loses any text-only signal that may help certain queries. Dual adapters add a small but non-zero training/serving overhead.

Fixes: Targets the noise-from-redundant-context problem of M3DocRAG, MDocAgent and MoLoRAG, where retrieved pages still contain large amounts of irrelevant content that confuses VLMs. Distinguishes itself from reasoning-enhancement methods (MACT) and retrieval-enhancement methods (MoLoRAG, ViDoRAG) by introducing context engineering as a third paradigm. Builds on Doc-V*'s coarse-to-fine inspection but operates training-free with an adaptive trajectory length scaling with K.
Key Technique: Training-free four-agent framework with coarse-to-fine context construction. (1) ColPali retriever narrows search space to top-K pages. (2) Clue Discovery Agent processes each retrieved page individually and extracts structured clues (region, content, key insight, reasoning trace) via VLM single-page reasoning, producing fine-grained textual evidence. (3) Page Screening Agent classifies each page as Completely Relevant / Relevant / Irrelevant via joint semantic-visual reasoning, filtering out distracting pages while preserving critical visual elements (tables, charts). (4) Difficulty Assessment Agent classifies query complexity (ordinary vs reasoning mode) and dispatches to the corresponding model type (Instruct vs Thinking). (5) Core Decision Agent synthesises the distilled context with mode-specific prompts to generate the answer with evidence references. Effective context length stays fixed regardless of K; recall scales with K without amplifying hallucinations.
Limitations: Heavy dependence on initial retriever recall; missed relevant pages cannot be recovered downstream. Pure prompt-driven design limits self-evolution and adaptation through experience. Heavy compute due to four-agent pipeline (Clue Discovery processes each retrieved page sequentially). Evaluated primarily on English long-document benchmarks; multilingual and handwritten documents untested. Difficulty assessment relies on a binary (ordinary/reasoning) classification that may oversimplify query complexity.

Fixes: Addresses the under-explored multimodal reranker space, where VLM rerankers lack instruction-following capability and explicit reasoning. Targets the limitation of using general-purpose VLMs as zero-shot rerankers without fine-tuning. Provides interpretability via Chain-of-Thought reasoning during reranking, contrasting with standard rerankers that output ranks without justification. Slots into the trained-retrieval-component family alongside DFVC and MoLoRAG retrieval scorer.
Key Technique: Two-stage reranker training pipeline. Stage 1: SFT on a dataset of 7,200 instances with structured reasoning paths constructed via GPT-4o on single-image sub-tasks (each page paired with the question for relevance reasoning); per-page reasoning statements are concatenated into a complete <think> chain, paired with the relevance-sorted <answer> ranking. Stage 2: GRPO RL on a resolution-balanced subset of 3,000 samples with two reward functions: a result reward (cube-rank-discounted top-K reward over correctly identified pages) and a format reward (structure validity + length accuracy + range validity). Resolution-balanced sampling across 10 partitions reduces gradient bias from imbalanced image sizes. The model jointly processes all candidate pages, generates an explicit reasoning chain for each, and outputs a final ranking. Achieved second place in WWW 2025 Multimodal RAG Challenge.
Limitations: Reranker only — relies on an upstream retriever (ColQwen) for candidate pages; cannot recover from poor retrieval. Trained and evaluated on MMDocIR; cross-domain generalisation to other multi-page corpora not extensively tested. Reasoning-chain output adds inference latency over rank-only rerankers. Reward design uses cube-rank discounting which may over-emphasise top positions at the cost of broader coverage. Limited to text-image rerank; no native handling of layout or table structure.

Fixes: Addresses the unimodal limitation of pure-text RAG (loses visual information) and pure-vision RAG (constrained by VLM visual reasoning capability and language bias). Extends single-document QA to multi-document settings with visually-rich content (tables, charts, slides). Differentiates from multi-document benchmarks (LongBench, Loong) that are text-only by introducing VisDoMBench with explicit multimodal content.
Key Technique: Parallel multimodal RAG with consistency-constrained modality fusion. (1) Textual RAG pipeline: OCR-based text extraction (PyTesseract), recursive chunking (3000 chars with 10% overlap), BGE-1.5 dense retrieval, three-step Evidence Curation + CoT + Answer Generation. (2) Visual RAG pipeline: ColPali/ColQwen2 page-level visual retrieval with the same three-step prompting strategy. (3) Modality Fusion: an LLM evaluates consistency between the textual and visual reasoning chains; on inconsistency, it reconciles differences and re-evaluates evidence to produce a coherent final answer. Releases VisDoMBench: 2,271 queries over 1,277 documents (~128.7 pages per query) sourced from PaperTab, FetaTab, SciGraphQA, SPIQA and SlideVQA with adversarial document augmentation and query disambiguation.
Limitations: Two parallel pipelines double indexing/inference cost. Modality fusion still uses a single LLM, so cross-modal disagreements may resolve incorrectly when both pipelines err. Evidence curation adds latency on top of standard RAG. Performance bound by quality of OCR (PyTesseract) for textual pipeline and by visual encoder for visual pipeline. Training-free design limits domain adaptation. Final answer synthesis may discard the better modality's evidence when consistency check fails.

Fixes: Targets the limitations of OCR-based RAG (cascading errors, layout/style loss) and prompt-only visual RAG (insufficient visual perception activation, repetitive ineffective retrieval calls). Improves over Search-R1-VL by adding a visual perception action space and a RAG-specific reward function. Aligns with Doc-V* in ReAct + RL training but adds explicit crop/zoom region selection rather than fetch-by-page-index.
Key Technique: Reinforcement learning framework for visual RAG built on Qwen2.5-VL (3B and 7B variants). Three innovations: (i) Visual perception action space — beyond search and answer, the agent emits <region> tags to select bounding boxes and crop+zoom into regions of interest, providing fine-grained perception within fixed token budgets; (ii) Multi-expert SFT trajectory data — large LMs guide the reasoning pathway while smaller expert models annotate region coordinates, scaling high-quality training data; (iii) Three-component reward — pattern reward (rule-based for action format), retrieval efficiency reward (modified NDCG over stacked retrieved images), and model-based outcome reward (LLM-evaluated answer correctness). Trained with GRPO on a search engine over ~70k visual documents, then evaluated on SlideVQA, ViDoSeek, and MMLongBench-Doc with model-based binary judgment. Outperforms Search-R1-VL by ~20-30% on Qwen2.5-VL backbones.
Limitations: Multi-turn reasoning increases inference latency (more search and perception turns than single-pass RAG). Relies on a fixed search engine over a pre-encoded image database. Evaluation uses model-based binary 0/1 judgment, making cross-system comparison with ANLS/EM/F1 metrics indirect. Visual perception is constrained to bounding-box cropping; no other actions like rotation. The expert annotation pipeline depends on grounding-specific models being available for the backbone family.

Fixes: Addresses both information interference from irrelevant content and quadratic Transformer cost in MLLMs for long documents, without external retrievers (avoiding error propagation between separately-trained retriever and generator). Improves over token compression (which sacrifices fine-grained details) and external-retrieval RAG (which lacks end-to-end optimisation) by integrating retrieval into the MLLM's early Transformer layers.
Key Technique: Unifies retrieval and generation within a single MLLM via a lightweight cross-modal retrieval module inserted at an early Transformer layer (layer 6 by default in Qwen2.5-VL). The module consists of two linear projections with GELU activation that map early-layer hidden states into a low-dimensional space, then computes ColBERT-style late-interaction relevance between page visual features and query text features. Top-k pages are retained in the hidden states; non-retained pages are discarded before deeper layers. Two-stage training: (i) pretrain the retrieval module with retrieval loss over positive/negative page pairs, (ii) jointly fine-tune with LoRA (rank 32) on both LLM and retrieval module using combined retrieval + generation losses. Supported by empirical analysis showing MLLMs naturally exhibit a coarse-to-fine attention pattern (uniform attention in early layers, focused on evidence pages in deeper layers).
Limitations: Effectiveness depends on the choice of insertion layer; the optimal position varies with the backbone (layer 6 for Qwen2.5-VL). Top-k is fixed (5 by default), creating a hard cap on evidence pages. The retrieval module operates over visual features only, so cross-modal alignment depends on textual queries having semantic overlap with vision features. Training on MPDocVQA + DUDE + SlideVQA may overfit (LongDocURL fine-tuning shows degradation in their experiments). Evaluation conducted only on standard benchmarks; long-tail document types not tested.

Fixes: Addresses two gaps in existing pure-vision MP-DocVQA approaches: (i) low signal-to-noise ratio in long documents where evidence is buried in irrelevant pages (worse for top-k RAG with large k for high recall vs small k risking missed evidence), and (ii) supervision scarcity — datasets typically provide only short final answers without intermediate localization or reasoning supervision. Improves over DocR1's EviGRPO by adding a more elaborate analyse-localise-reason workflow with explicit page identifiers, plus an Evidence-Guided Resolution Allocation strategy to enable training on longer documents within memory constraints.
Key Technique: MLLM-Centric Adaptation Model (Qwen-2.5-VL-7B-Instruct) trained with the Analysis-Localization-Reasoning (ALR) paradigm. Each page's visual tokens are prefixed with a textual page identifier (e.g., 'Page i') to enable explicit grounding. The model emits structured <think><answer> outputs with three sub-stages: Question Analysis, Evidence Localization (referencing specific page IDs), and Reasoning Process. Two-stage training: (i) SFT on 13,986 high-quality ALR CoT samples distilled from Gemini-2.5-Flash using minimal-context teacher prompts (only ground-truth evidence pages plus a few distractors) with two-stage EM + GPT-4o verification; (ii) Evidence-aware GRPO (EviGRPO) jointly optimising format reward + weighted F1 evidence reward (recall-favoured) + ANLS answer reward. Evidence-Guided Resolution Allocation (EGRA) downsamples 70% of non-evidence pages to lower resolution during training to fit longer documents in GPU memory while strengthening signal. Achieves 30-60% gains over the Qwen2.5-VL-7B baseline across five MP-VRDU benchmarks.
Limitations: Trained only on MP-DocVQA + DUDE (≤20 pages), with OOD generalisation to longer documents (MMLongBench-Doc avg 47 pages) demonstrated empirically but not guaranteed for ultra-long documents. EGRA's resolution downsampling is heuristic (70%/30% split, fixed ratios); adaptive resolution allocation not explored. Inference uses fixed 1024×784 resolution, bounding maximum context; very long documents may still exceed memory budget. Reasoning chain output adds inference latency vs direct answer. Performance gap remains versus closed-source GPT-4o on the most challenging benchmarks.

Fixes: Addresses the structural blindness of conventional chunk-based RAG, which destroys document hierarchy (sections, page references, figure-text alignment) when flattening to disconnected chunks. Improves over MDocAgent and other multi-agent systems that retrieve at page level by introducing an explicit document-level hierarchical structure as a first-class retrieval target. Aligns with MultiDocFusion in using document hierarchy but extends to multimodal elements (figures, tables, charts) and adds a reflective reasoning loop.
Key Technique: Two-agent framework. (i) Structured-Semantic Understanding Agent (SSUA) parses each document via pymupdf and pdffigure2 into a hierarchical Document MAP (DMAP) with section-level hierarchy, page-level aggregation, and element-level representation (page_content, figures, tables, charts), each with both visual and textual descriptions. The DMAP captures intra-page semantics and inter-page references. (ii) Reflective Reasoning Agent (RRA) performs tri-path retrieval over DMAP — Structured Semantic Retrieval (LLM navigates DMAP summary tree), Textual Feature Retrieval (ColBERTv2), and Visual Feature Retrieval (ColPali) — then fuses retrieved elements and generates an answer via MDocAgent. A reflective loop assesses answer completeness via LLM evaluator and triggers additional DMAP traversal (neighbouring or parent elements) when evidence is insufficient. Evaluated on MMLongBench-Doc, LongDocURL, PaperTab, PaperText, FetaTab using GPT-4o binary judgment; outperforms MDocAgent baseline by 12.4% average accuracy.
Limitations: Performance depends on parser quality for DMAP construction (pymupdf for text, pdffigure2 for figures). DMAP construction is per-document offline cost, which scales with document count. The reflection loop adds inference latency, especially on unanswerable questions (MMLongBench-Doc requires the most reflection ops). Backbone is closed-source (GPT-4o or Qwen-plus); MDocAgent generator inherits its limitations. Evaluation uses GPT-4o binary judgment, making cross-system comparison with ANLS/EM/F1 metrics indirect. Tested only on MMDocQA-style benchmarks, not standard MP-DocVQA / DUDE / SlideVQA.
